title: [BUG] Streaming I/O Workbook commit never resolve with Stream Passthrough
author: shujer
body:
## 🐛 Bug Report

<!-- A clear and concise description of what the bug is. -->

Lib version: 4.3.2

## Steps To Reproduce

<!-- The exact steps required to reproduce the issue, ideally with a code example -->

```javascript
import * as ExcelJS from 'exceljs'
import { Stream, PassThrough } from 'stream'
const stream = new Stream.Duplex.PassThrough({})
const wb = new ExcelJS.stream.xlsx.WorkbookWriter({
      stream: this.stream,
      useStyles: true,
      useSharedStrings: true
    })
const ws = wb.addWorksheet('XYZ');

ws.addRow({}).commit()
ws.commit()
await wb.commit()
```

## The expected behaviour:

<!-- A clear and concise description of what you expected to happen. -->
Promise be resolved

## Possible solution (optional, but very helpful):

not sure why, any code example for this case? seems finish event not triggered so that commit can't resolved.
Background: 
I want to use Stream PassThrough as I can directly to upload  the stream to S3 with sdk

comments.author: kumaresansv
comments.body: I am also facing similar issue when streaming directly to GCP. Is there a resolution for this? Did you find any workaround?

comments.author: kumaresansv
comments.body:
I did see a difference in behavior when I updated my default GCP credentials(that got expired) locally. I was not able to create any file on GCP for many of my runs. In all of those runs, the call workbook.commit() never resolved. It almost felt like that the issue was in the workbook.commit() step. 
Once I updated my credentials, everything started to work again. I am keeping my fingers crossed for the issue to be associated with expired credentials.

Can you check if you also have similar issue?

comments.author: zlooun
comments.body:
So much time has passed, and no one wants to solve such a banal and at the same time important problem. Why waste time on steam if it doesn't work properly anyway?

Solve this problem pls

comments.author: akshayhubilo
comments.body: Its been more than a year, and this issue has still not been resolved. Any timeline?

comments.author: AmberWilkie
comments.body: Same problem... this wasn't an issue for me until I sent my code to a dev environment to test and it just got hung up only sometimes in the most frustrating way. Based on logging, it also appears to be related to `workbook.commit()` - which TS won't let me await because it's not async (same for `addRow`).

comments.author: julesblancphin
comments.body: Hi, this has now been open for two years and still hasn't been fixed. Any timeline?

comments.author: AmberWilkie
comments.body:
I just published this article / tutorial I've had in draft for ages (and was probably going to give up on) based on your ping @julesblancphin . I believe most of the internet is doing exceljs + streams wrong, including the examples in this issue. I was able to resolve these problems by writing code like I have here: https://medium.com/@heyamberwilkie/stream-exceljs-notebook-to-s3-in-typescript-43799cc3bd75

I hope that's useful to you or anyone else.

The other super useful thing to know about these technologies is that any little thing that goes wrong with the workbook (like having too many characters in a sheet name) will cause malformatting, which can look very much like the stream was terminated early or didn't get the right data. When troubleshooting, always look for problems on the exceljs side first because streams can take you down a mad rabbithole. Every time a problem like that cropped up with my project, it was because of the inputs, not anything with streams.

comments.author: julesblancphin
ian-weir
comments.body: @AmberWilkie @julesblancphin Thank you for the input, mine was similar. I had actually found Amber's article before I was running into my issue and what solved it for me was using `Promise.allSettled(...)` and passing workbook.commit() and the function that completes the upload ( `upload.done()` for s3 in my case). Not sure if this is specific to S3 but it seems like awaiting one before the other caused some sort of deadlock where the promise wouldn't resolve.
